questions.question: What does it mean to say an algorithm runs in O(n) time?
options: Its running time grows at most proportionally to the input size
It always performs exactly n operations regardless of input
It runs faster than any O(log n) algorithm
Its memory use is proportional to the input size
questions.explanation: Big-O is an upper bound on growth rate; O(n) means time grows at most linearly. It is about time here (not memory), is not an exact count, and says nothing about beating O(log n).

questions.question: Which statement best characterises the difference between O(n log n) and O(n^2) for large inputs?
options: O(n log n) grows much more slowly than O(n^2) as n increases
They grow at the same rate once n is large enough
O(n^2) is always preferred because it is simpler to implement
O(n log n) uses more memory, so it is effectively slower
questions.explanation: For large n, n log n is far below n^2. The others confuse growth rate with implementation simplicity or memory.

questions.question: A loop runs from i = 1 to n, and for each i an inner loop runs from j = 1 to i. What is the tightest Big-O of the total number of inner iterations?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
questions.explanation: Total iterations = 1+2+...+n = n(n+1)/2, which is O(n^2). It is not linear; the triangular sum dominates.

questions.question: Doubling the input size makes a function take four times as long. Which complexity class best fits this behaviour?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
questions.explanation: If t(2n) = 4 t(n), the cost scales with the square of the input, i.e. O(n^2). Linear would double; exponential would square the runtime itself.

questions.question: Two algorithms solve the same problem: one is O(n) and one is O(n^2). For a very small, fixed input, which is guaranteed to be faster?
options: Neither — Big-O does not guarantee the winner on small inputs
The O(n) algorithm, because lower Big-O always means faster
The O(n^2) algorithm, because it does more work per step
Both are identical in speed for any input size
questions.explanation: Big-O describes asymptotic growth and hides constants; for small n a higher-order algorithm can win. The 'lower Big-O always wins' claim ignores constants and small-n behaviour.

questions.question: A student claims their algorithm is O(n) but it contains a nested loop where the inner loop runs a fixed 100 times. Why is the O(n) claim actually correct?
options: The inner loop is a constant factor, so total work is 100n = O(n)
The claim is wrong; any nested loop makes it O(n^2)
It is O(n) only if 100 is larger than n
Nested loops are ignored when computing Big-O
questions.explanation: A loop bounded by a constant contributes a constant factor, which Big-O drops, leaving O(n). Nesting matters only when the inner bound grows with n.

questions.question: You must pick a sort for an array that is almost always nearly sorted, with occasional large random shuffles, on memory-constrained hardware. Which choice is the most defensible and why?
options: Insertion sort, because it is near-linear on nearly-sorted data and uses O(1) extra space
Merge sort, because its worst case is O(n log n) regardless of input
Quicksort with a fixed first-element pivot, because it is fast on average
Bubble sort, because it is the simplest to reason about
questions.explanation: Nearly-sorted + tight memory favours insertion sort (adaptive, in-place). Merge sort needs O(n) extra space; fixed-pivot quicksort degrades on structured data; bubble sort is uniformly poor.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a hash function in a hash table?
options: To map a key to a bucket index in the underlying array
To sort the keys so lookups can use binary search
To compress the stored values to save memory
To encrypt the keys so they cannot be read
questions.explanation: A hash function turns a key into an index. It does not sort, compress, or (in general) encrypt — those are different concerns.

questions.question: In hashing, what is a 'collision'?
options: Two distinct keys hash to the same bucket index
A key is inserted twice with different values
The table runs out of memory during insertion
A lookup is performed on a key that was never inserted
questions.explanation: A collision is two different keys mapping to the same slot. The other options describe duplicate inserts, allocation failure, and a missing-key lookup.

questions.question: A hash table uses h(k) = k mod 8. Where does key 27 land?
options: Bucket 3
Bucket 7
Bucket 8
Bucket 2
questions.explanation: 27 mod 8 = 3. A table of size 8 has buckets 0..7, so bucket 8 cannot exist.

questions.question: With separate chaining and a good hash function, the average-case time for a successful lookup is closest to which of the following?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
questions.explanation: With a low, bounded load factor and uniform hashing, expected chain length is constant, so lookup is O(1) on average. O(n) is the degenerate worst case.

questions.question: Why does a hash table typically resize (grow its array) once the load factor gets high?
options: To keep average chain length low so operations stay near O(1)
To put the keys back into sorted order
To reduce the total memory used by the table
To change the hash function to a more secure one
questions.explanation: Growing the array lowers the load factor, keeping chains short and operations near constant time. Resizing uses more memory, not less, and does not sort or re-secure keys.

questions.question: Two hash table designs handle collisions differently: A uses separate chaining, B uses open addressing with linear probing. Under a high load factor, why does B tend to degrade more sharply?
options: Linear probing causes primary clustering, so probe sequences lengthen quickly
Chaining cannot store more items than the array size
Open addressing recomputes the hash on every probe, which is slow
Chaining keeps all items in one bucket, so it is always faster
questions.explanation: Linear probing suffers primary clustering: filled runs grow and lengthen future probes, degrading fast as the table fills. Chaining is not capped by array size, and the other claims are false.

questions.question: You are designing a hash table for an adversarial web service where attackers can choose the keys they submit. Which design decision most directly defends worst-case performance, and why?
options: Use a randomised/keyed hash (e.g., SipHash) so attackers cannot force collisions
Increase the initial array size once at startup and never resize again
Switch from chaining to linear probing to save memory
Pick a simple modulo hash because it is fastest to compute
questions.explanation: Attacker-chosen keys can force collisions and O(n) chains; a keyed/randomised hash makes the bucket mapping unpredictable. A fixed size, probing, or a trivial modulo hash all leave the collision attack open.
